notes: Also: who(polite) donata どなた. polite

Also: nande なんで[何で]; doushite どうして [如何して] (UK) There are three different words for why, and you may be asking yourself which one is best to use. Naze なぜ is the most common, while nande なんで[何で] is like the Japanese version of what for, or, how come? Doushite どうして[如何して] is the polite version of why.
Also: how (polite) ikaga いかが [如何] (UK). polite

english: which out of three or more choices

notes: Also: how old ikutsu いくつ [幾つ] (UK)
Also: yeah un うん; yeah ee ええ; no iie いいえ; nah uun ううん

romaji: Moshi moshi (some people pronounce this mushi mushi)

hiragana: よろしくおねがいします/ おねがいします
げんきです/ まあまあです
どうもありがとうございます
どういたしまして/ いいえいいえ

notes: Also: boku ぼく[僕]; watashi わたし[私]; watakushi わたくし [私]
Also: anata あなた [貴方] (UK) 3. you (rude) omae おまえ[お前]; kisama きさま[貴様]. rude
Also: boyfriend kare かれ [彼]
Also: girlfriend kanojo かのじょ[彼女]
Also: ra ら
Also: girl suffix chan ちゃん
Also: doctor suffix sensei せんせい [先生]

Also: junior suffix kouhai こうはい [後輩] Since we don't know any verbs yet, having example sentences isn't the best way to practice these words. What I want you to do is: go about your day, and if you see someone, try to think of the appropriate pronoun for them, and also think of the suffix that you would attach to their name. This will be great practice!
Also: people hitobito ひとびと[人々]; (humble) person kata かた [方]. humble
Also: mina みな[皆] This word has two spellings, but is most always paired with san さん, and when it is, will take the shorter spelling: minasan みなさん[皆さん]
Also: physician ishi いし[医師]
Also: teacher kyouju きょうじゅ[教授]
Also: omawarisan おまわりさん Omawarisan おまわりさん is a friendly word for police often used by children.
Also: woman ooeru オーエル [OL] As you can see, these words are taken from English. OL stands for Office Lady.

hiragana: ぼくはにほんじんじゃない
かのじょはちゅうごくごをはなせます
かれはかんこくからきました
えいごをはなせますか
あなたはドイツじんですか

romaji: Amerika de wa eigo ga hanasareru
hiragana: アメリカではえいごがはなされる

english: What kind of country is Japan?
romaji: Nihon wa donna kuni desu ka?
hiragana: にほんはどんなくにですか
わたしはがいこくじんです
ぼくはりゅうがくせいです

romaji: Kaigai ni itta koto wa arimasu ka?
hiragana: かいがいにいったことはありますか

notes: Also: foot ashi あし [足] In Japanese, the leg and foot are one unit.
Also: toe ashiyubi あしゆび[足指] In Japanese, for toes, they literally say foot finger.
Mouth in Japanese can refer to many types of openings. For example: deguchi でぐち[出口](exit).

romaji: Migi no ude ga itai desu
hiragana: みぎのうでうでがいたいです

english: As for my leg, it's alright
hiragana: あしはだいじょうぶです
あしゆびもだいじょうぶです

english: As for my ears, they are okay
hiragana: みみはだいじょうぶです

english: As for my mouth, it's okay
hiragana: くちはだいじょうぶです

notes: Also: gohan ごはん[ご飯] Kome refers to the rice itself. Gohan refers to a bowl of rice.

Also: choushoku ちょうしょく[朝食] Japanese people traditionally eat rice with every meal, therefore morning rice bowl became the word for breakfast. The second word, choushoku means early meal, and is used more often to refer to anything that is eaten in the morning. The same rule applies to the next two words.
Also: chuushoku ちゅうしょく[昼食]
Also: yuushoku ゆうしょく[夕食]

english: I buy food at the supermarket
hiragana: スーパーでたべものをかいます

english: As for drinks, I buy them from a vending machine
hiragana: のみものはじどうはんばいきでかいます

english: I eat meat with my hands
I eat vegetables with a fork
hiragana: フォークでやさいをたべます

english: I buy fruits at the vegetable store
hiragana: やおやでくだものをかいます

english: I cook rice without a pot
romaji: Nabe nashi de kome o takimasu
hiragana: なべなしでこめをたきます

english: I eat breakfast at a cafe
hiragana: カフェでちょうしょくをたべます
うちでちゅうしょくをたべます

english: I eat dinner at a restaurant
hiragana: レストランでゆうしょくをたべます

romaji: Hashi nashi de gohan o tabemasu
hiragana: はしなしでごはんをたべます

notes: Japan is crowded, so instead of having central police stations, they have small scattered police stations called boxes throughout the cities.
Also: ie いえ[家] Uchi refers to your home. Ie refers to someone else's home and also the physical building itself. Don't confuse this word with iie いいえ(no).
Also: city shi し[市] Machi can mean town or city. Shi is the suffix used for cities. For example, Yokohama City is pronounced Yokohama-shi.
Also: dish osara おさら[お皿] This can also refer to bowls, any type of dish.
This is a small bowl used for rice or tea. The word itself actually means tea bowl.
Also: middle naka なか[中]
Also: before mae まえ[前] This word is also used with time.
Also: father tousan とうさん[父さん]
Also: mother kaasan かあさん[母さん]
Also: older brother niisan にいさん[兄さん]
Also: older sister neesan ねえさん[姉さん]
Also: younger brother otoutosan おとうとさん[弟さん]
Also: younger sister imoutosan いもうとさん[妹さん]
Also: grandfather jiisan じいさん[爺さん]
Also: grandmother baasan ばあさん[婆さん]
Also: your husband goshujin ごしゅじん[ご主人]
Also: your wife okusan おくさん[ 奥さん]
Also: your son musukosan むすこさん[ 息子さん]
Also: your daughter ojousan おじょうさん[お嬢さん]
Also: your grandchild omagosan おまごさん[お孫さん]
Also: your uncle ojisan おじさん[叔父さん] Don't confuse this word with grandfather.
Also: your aunt obasan おばさん[叔母さん] Don't confuse this word with grandmother.

Also: 従兄; 従弟; 従姉妹; 従姉; 従妹] (UK) The kanji for this word depends on the gender of the cousin. It can be written six different ways, but is always pronounced the same. The word is usually just written without kanji, however. The order in the brackets above is as follows: male cousins, older male cousin, younger male cousin, female cousins, older female cousin, younger female cousin.

Also: 兄妹; 姉妹; 姉弟] Similar to cousin, the kanji for this word can be written four different ways, however, the kanji for two brothers is most often used, even when referring to women. The order above is male siblings, mixed siblings with younger sister, female siblings, mixed siblings with younger brother.
Here, the no の is the ownership particle.

hiragana: おっとはなまけものだ
おくさんはしんせつです

english: My son is a hard worker
hiragana: むすこははたらきものです

romaji: Musume wa atama ga ii desu
hiragana: むすめはあたまがいいです
おじさんはしかいですか
おばさんはいしゃですか
たくさんいとこがいます
きょうだいはときどきけんかします

english: My brother in law is rich
romaji: Giri no ani wa okane mochi desu
hiragana: ぎりのあにはおかねもちです

notes: Also: cell phone keetai ケータイ[携帯] Cell phone is a rare word that uses sometimes uses katakana. The kanji doesn't refer to a cell phone specifically, but rather, anything carried in your hand.
Also: Wait, what? Are? あれ。
Also: uhh ano あの; etto えっと
Also: jaa じゃあ These are used at the beginning of sentences just like English when you are changing the subject or making a decision.
Similarly to saa さあ and jaa じゃあ this will be used at the beginning of a sentence when you are ready to move on to some new topic.
Also: luggage nimotsu にもつ[荷物] This often refers to luggage but also just things you are carrying or taking with you somewhere.
Also: clock tokei とけい[時計]
This refers to an electric train, opposed to a steam engine. Most all public trains in Japan are electric.
suffix
suffix
suffix
Also: several ikutsumo いくつも
Also: any how doudemo どうでも
Also: not often amari あまり This word will also be used with a negative verb, to create a double negative sentence. It can also be pronounced as anmari あんまり.
This refers to temples for the Buddhist religion.
Shinto is a religion that is native to Japan, that was popular before Buddhism. Though most Japanese people are non religious, it is still customary to go to shrines and temples on certain occasions.
Also: port minato みなと[港]
If you have to compare this to something in western culture, it's similar to a bed and breakfast.
Also: district gun ぐん[郡] Wards are used for large cities, while districts are used for smaller cities.
If you notice, this kanji is two trees.
This kanji is three trees, an even bigger amount!
Also: atmosphere kuuki くうき[空気]
Also: class jugyou じゅぎょう[授業]
Also: pupil seito せいと[生徒] This refers to any type of student, whereas gakusei がくせい[学生] refers to a school student.

Also: kotoba ことば[言葉] Tango たんご[単語] refers to vocabulary and individual words. Kotoba ことば[言葉] refers to a language or a dialect. For example: I don't understand your words, is an expression that we don't often use in English, we would say something like I don't understand what you are saying. But in Japanese, they will use this expression with kotoba ことば[言葉].
Also: essay sakubun さくぶん[作文]

Also: article bunshou ぶんしょう[文章] This can refer to someone's writing style, as well as any small composition, as in, a collection of sentences. The difference between this and sakubun さくぶん[作文] is the length.
Also: drawing; painting e え[絵]
Also: film eiga えいが[映画]
A postcard with a picture is a combination of this word and picture: ehagaki えはがき[絵葉書].
Also: stream kawa かわ[川; 河] The first kanji is for river, the second for stream.

The kanji for this word literally mean hand washing. You can also use the word borrowed from English, toire トイレ. Also, most Japanese homes separate the toilet from the room with the bath, which is called furoba ふろば[風呂場].
Also: entry hall genkan げんかん[玄関] This refers to the entrance itself as well as the area around the entrance.
This refers to a Japanese style sliding door.

english: to see / to look / to watch
notes: Also: to look; to watch miru みる[見る; 観る] (I) The first kanji is the general word for see, while the second is used to write watch as part of the audience, for instance, in a theater or sports arena. As you continue your studies, you will find many words like this, that have different kanji for different meanings of the same word.
This verb almost always refers to returning home.
This verb implies working hard. You can not use it to say you work for a company.
This is the only verb that ends in nuぬ. It conjugates just like muむ verbs.

english: to say / to tell / to be called
notes: Also: to tell; to be called iu いう[言う] In Japanese, say and tell are the same word. If you want to say tell, use a noun + niに to show who or what you are telling something to. We can also use this with names + toと to say I'm called... If you remember back in Day 3, we learned how to say our names using to moushimasuともうします[と申します] . Mousuもうす[申す] is the humble form of iuいう[言う]

Also: to listen kiku きく[聞く] Ask and listen are also the same word. You should be able to understand which one it is from context. In general, but not always, ask will use the particle niに and listen will use the particle oを.
The kanji for this word will often be used for types books or written things, or as a suffix to indicate a written version. In these cases it is pronounced sho しょ[書].

english: Are you coming to the party?

hiragana: いつか、みんなしにます
おかあさんにいいます

english: I will listen to your story
hiragana: あなたのはなしをききます
コーヒーをのみますか

english: I will talk to him later
hiragana: あとでかれとしゃべります
おきゃくはたべものをたのみます

english: Do you use a computer for work?
romaji: Shigoto wa pasokon o tsukaimasu ka?
hiragana: しごとはぱそこんをつかいますか
このしつもんにこたえますか

english: to sleep / to go to bed / to lie down
notes: Also: to go to bed; to lie down neru ねる[寝る] (I) The verb to sleep is actually nemuru ねむる[眠る], but neru ねる[寝る] is used far more often.
This verb also means to rouse.

english: to wake up / to open your eyes
notes: Also: to open your eyes samasu さます[覚ます] This literally means to open your eyes, not getting out of bed, and it usually paired with the word me め[目](eye). It is also used to say sober up, as in, the opposite of drunk.
Also: to know wakaru わかる[分かる] Similar to saying I dunno in English, many speakers will drop the raら and say this negatively as wakanai わかない instead of wakaranai わからない.
Also: to misplace nakusu なくす[無くす]

english: to turn off / to extinguish / to erase
notes: Also: to extinguish; to erase kesu けす[消す] The intransitive version of this verb is kieru きえる[消える].
Also: to create tsukuru つくる[作る]
An alternate older pronunciation of this is yuku ゆく, but it is almost never used.
Also: to get on noru のる[乗る]
Also: to polish migaku みがく[磨く]
Also: to invite yobu よぶ[呼ぶ]
Also: to suck suu すう[吸う]
Also: 撮る] The second kanji is used for taking pictures.
Also: to search sagasu さがす[探す]
Investigate is a bit formal in English. This word is more casual in Japanese, and can mean to search for information or find out. This word is also used to say look up words.

english: to enjoy oneself / to have fun
notes: Also: to have fun tanoshimu たのしむ[楽しむ] You can use the noun tanoshimi たのしみ[楽しみ] to say to look forward to. For example, the phrase I'm looking forward to it: Tanoshimi desu. たのしみです。[楽しみです。]
You will see the intransitive form of this word a lot at the end of television shows: tsuzuku つづく[続く] (to be continued)

Also: to return modoru もどる[戻る] This is often interchangeable with kaeru かえる[帰る](to return). The difference is that this word means simply to go back from where you came, as opposed to going back home.
This word is also used for seat belts, as in fasten.
Also: to be wrong chigau ちがう[違う] Be careful with this word. It is used to say that two things are different, as in not the same, and also used to say that something is wrong, as in not correct.
Also: to film toru とる[撮る]

Also: naru なる[鳴る] (inanimate). animate. inanimate Chirp is chosen for this word because the kanji depicts the mouth of a bird, however it is used for many different animals noises, as well as beeping electronics. When used for electronics, it will be the inanimate version, naru なる[鳴る].

english: to stick / to glue / to attach
notes: Also: to glue; to attach haru はる[貼る]
This is used for the weather, referring to rain or snow falling.

english: to go for a walk do walk sanpo

notes: Also: to unite au あう[合う] Be very careful with this word, not only is it a homonym with au あう[会う](to meet), but the kanji look very similar as well.

english: to take out / to get out / to put out
notes: Also: to get out; to put out dasu だす[出す] This verb is the transitive form of deru でる[出る](to exit). It is used in many contexts, whenever you are putting or taking something out. For example, it can mean to submit, to reveal, to turn in, to publish, to send, to produce.

english: to take out / to pull out
notes: Also: to pull out hikidasu ひきだす[引き出す] This is the word for literally taking something out of a container. It is not used in many other contexts like dasu だす[出す].

english: to be packed put into / to be crowded
notes: Also: to be crowded komu こむ[込む; 混む] The first kanji is for packed, the second for crowded. This first kanji is used for many compound verbs, and it has a sense of inclusion. The second kanji meaning is crowded or mixture.
Also: to insert kumikomu くみこむ[組み込む] The first kanji means association or unite. So this word has the sense of putting things together.
A secondary meaning of this word is to contain. When people ask what ingredients were used to make a food, they will literally ask What has entered this dish?
A secondary meaning of this word is to tell. As in, when you are telling someone some information, you are teaching them something they didn't know before.
Also: to insert ireru いれる[入れる] (I)
This is limited to instruments that don't involve blowing air.
This is used for playing wind instruments, as well as to say that things are blowing, such as the wind.
Also: to end owaru おわる[終わる] The transitive form of this verb is oeru おえる[終える].

This word is a bit complicated. The core meaning of this word is more along the lines of to put out. We get the meaning shine from the idea that the sun is putting out light. This word can mean to wear with swords (more important back in the day than now). It can mean to serve / to offer, as in a cup of tea. It can mean to put your arms out, and also it is used for putting up an umbrella.

Te-Iru There is a lot of confusion between this verb and wakaru わかる[分かる]. Shiru is used to say that some information is in your head at this moment. This is why it is always used in the continuous tense, you are literally saying that you are in the state of holding the information. In the negative, you are not talking about the state of your mind, but rather, you are saying That information hasn't entered my brain. This is why it is not in the continuous tense in the negative. Wakaru is used to
Also: to reside sumu すむ[住む] (Te-Iru). Te-Iru

Also: to possess motsu もつ[持つ] (Te-Iru). Te-Iru This can sometimes be interchanged with aru ある to say that you have something. The emphasis with this verb is that you are currently in possession of it, you are holding it.
Also: to memorize oboeru おぼえる[覚える] (I) (Te-Iru). Te-Iru The emphasis on this word is information going into your head, as well as being in the state of being able to recall the information.
Also: to recall omoidasu おもいだす[思い出す] The emphasis on this word is the information coming out of your head. This compound verb literally means exiting memory.
Te-Iru In English there is the comical distinction between loving someone and being in love with someone. This word means in love.
Te-Iru To say that you are married, you will use the Te-Iru form of this verb. If you use the simple present tense, you are asking someone to marry you, so watch out!
Also: at; as tsutomeru つとめる[勤める] (I)(Te-Iru). Te-Iru This verb literally means to serve. With companies use the particle niに, with types of jobs, use the particle oを.
Te-Iru

english: to be similar / to look the same
notes: Also: to look the same niru にる[似る] (I)(Te-Iru). Te-Iru
Instead of take, in Japanese we say enter a bath.
Also: lock kagi かぎ[鍵] This word is used for both the lock and your keys. To say that something is locked or unlocked, you will use the words open and closed: akuあく[開く] and shimaru しまる[閉まる].
Also: basket; purse kaban かばん[鞄]
People will often say this twice, zehi zehi ぜひぜひ. It is not used to modify verbs like certainly in English, but rather, as a reply, to say that you will certainly do something.
Also: certain tashika たしか[確か] Tashika たしか[確か] is often used with the particle niに.
This is used to say an exact amount, as in money or time. In English we can sometimes respond to something, showing our agreement by saying, Exactly! But this word can not be used in that context.
Also: diligently chanto ちゃんと This word is often used with commands to tell someone to do something properly, as in, give a real effort.

Also: a little bit chotto ちょっと This word is used often, and in a lot of contexts. In the expression chotto matte ちょっとまって[ちょっと待って] is means please wait a minute. You can say chotto dake ちょっとだけ to say just a little bit. You can use it with adjectives to say something was somewhat, or little bit. Finally, you can say this word by itself in anger or frustration to say something like: Hey! What are you doing? What's wrong with you?
Also: certainly kanarazu かならず[必ず] This is often used to tell someone to make certain that they do something.

english: not necessarily / not always the case
notes: Also: not always the case kanarazushimo かならずしも[必ずしも] This is the opposite of kanarazu かならず[必ず].

Also: subtle bimyou びみょう[微妙] (Na) This word is often used idiomatically by itself to say: I'm not entirely certain what your saying is true, or, What you just said has raised more questions than answers. What the speaker is saying, is that the situation has become delicate, because it would be impolite to ask certain questions to get the truthful answers.
Also: dining hall shokudou しょくどう[食堂]
Also: travel ryokou りょこう[旅行] This can be paired with suru する to mean to go on on a trip.

Also: power denki でんき[電気] Instead of saying the name of the electric device, many Japanese people will just talk about turning on the electricity of the device. So instead of saying, Turn on the lights, they will say Turn on the electricity.
Also: really like daisuki だいすき[大好き]
Also: really hate daikirai だいきらい[大嫌い]
Also: overcoat uwagi うわぎ[上着]
Also: wood ki き[木]
Like the ending for people, you can add san さん to this word, as well as other small stores.

english: to receive / to get / to take a test
notes: Also: to get; to take a test ukeru うける[受ける] (I) This word's core meaning is to receive, but has a lot of uses in this context. It is also used in many compound verbs, as we will soon see. It is not usually used for obtaining physical objects, but rather, in sentences like, The speech was well received. Giving and receiving is actually a bit more complicated and is covered in detail in Volume Two.

Also: to accept uketoru うけとる[受け取る] This is a compound verb with toru とる[取る](to take). This verb shows a more active roll on the subjects part. You're not just passively receiving something, but you are receiving it with intent, as the verb to accept shows that the receiver must be willing.

Also: to agree ukeireru うけいれる[受け入れる] (I) This is paired with ireru いれる[入れる](to enter) to show that you have taken in something. It's interchangeable in many contexts with the previous word, but is more often used to show that you agree with something, you accept it as fact.

Also: to take uketsukeru うけつける[受け付ける] (I) This word is paired with tsukeru つける[付ける](to attach) and is usually used for paperwork and information, as in accepting applications. The noun uketsuke うけつけ[受け付け] means reception or front desk.

This is paired with motsu もつ[持つ](to hold). You've taken something in and now you are holding it, you are in charge of it. It is most often used as the noun, or No-Adjective, ukemochi うけもち[受け持ち] paired with a person who is in charge.
This is the most general form of breaking something. The intransitive form is kowareru こわれる[壊れる]

english: to fold / to snap / to break
notes: Also: to snap; to break oru おる[折る] The kanji here means to fold, and this word is often used to say that something broke in half.
Also: to defeat taosu たおす[倒す] This is often used to say that someone defeated someone else, as in a fight. The intransitive version is taoreru たおれる[倒れる]

english: to break / to divide / to crack
notes: Also: to divide; to crack waru わる[割る] This is the same word as mathematical division. It can also mean to split, to cut, to slice. The kanji for this word has the symbol for a sword on the right, so that may help you remember the meaning.

english: to break / to stop working koshou
notes: Also: to stop working koshou suru こしょうする[故障する] This is the word you will use for machines or devices when they stop working.
Also: trousers zubon ズボン
Also: onaka おなか[お腹]; i い[胃]
Also: to diminish heru へる[減る] (G)
Also: full of ippai いっぱい
This can also be used for sweet snacks, and it interchangeable with ame あめ[飴] in a lot of cases.
In Western countries, sake refers to only rice wine, but in Japan, it refers to any type of alcohol.
We can add the word meat to this to create pork: butaniku ぶたにく[豚肉]. In fact, we can do this with all animals to talk about the meat from that animal.
Also: gyuu ぎゅう[牛] For cows, ushi うし[牛] is the animal, while gyuu ぎゅう[牛] is used to make words like beef, gyuuniku ぎゅうにく[牛肉] and milk: gyuunyuu ぎゅうにゅう[牛乳]
Also: elementary school student shougakusei しょうがくせい[小学生]
Also: middle school student chuugakusei ちゅうがくせい[中学生]

Also: high school student student koukousei こうこうせい[高校生] If you compare the kanji in this word to the others, you can see why it has a different pronunciation. A less common version of this word is koutougakkou こうとうがっこう[高等学校]. You can see in this alternate word, that it maintains the gakkou pronunciation.
Also: university student daigakusei だいがくせい[大学生] Again, compare the kanji in this word to the others for pronunciation.
The noun nyuugaku means matriculation or enrollment.
Like the words for school and student, you can combine the prefixes with sotsu そつ[卒] to say that you are a graduate of that school. For example, high school graduate: kousotsu こうそつ[高卒].
Also: chemistry kagaku かがく[科学; 化学] These words are homonyms but have different kanji.
In English we use the verb take with medicine, in Japanese they usually use the word nomu のむ[飲む](to drink).
In English we say get or catch, in Japanese they use hiku ひく[引く](to pull).
Also: health guai ぐあい[具合]
Also: stature se せ[背] This word is used for the height of humans, not buildings or mountains.
This word can be used for any length, not just humans.

To say that you are sick, don't use this word, it is used for more serious diseases and can be translated to mean that you have an STD. To say that you are sick, say that your condition is bad by using guai ぐあい[具合](condition / health).
The noun sentaku せんたく[洗濯] by itself means laundry.
Also: residence juusho じゅうしょ[住所]
Also: form; figure katachi かたち[形]
Also: ○] The second kanji isn't a kanji, it's a circle. It's there because in written Japanese, they often use circles to express the word correct.
Again, not a kanji, but a mark used to say incorrect.
Literally this means three angles.
As you can see, the shapes are made by saying a number, and then the word angle. So of course, you already know a pentagon is gokaku ごかく[五角].
Also: interpret yakusu やくす[訳す] Interpreters are different from translators in that their job is to convey the core meaning of the original speakers intent, rather than a word for word translation.
This can be paired with suruする to make the verb to translate.
Also: things shinamono しなもの[品物] This usually refers to things being sold, a store's goods.
Also: to vanish kieru きえる[消える] (I) This doesn't mean to go outside, but rather to go off, as in the lights. This verb is intransitive.
To say happy birthday you will add omedetou gozaimasu おめでとうございます to the end.

Also: book case honbako ほんばこ[本箱] I bet you don’t know the difference between a book shelf and a book case! Don’t worry, no one knows in Japanese either. If we deconstruct the kanji, bookshelf literally means book ledge and book case literally means book box.

english: to attach / to join / to add
notes: Also: to join; to add tsukeru つける[付ける] (I) This verb can be used in many contexts where you are attaching or joining things together. It is used more often in compound verbs than by itself.
Also: coffee shop kissaten きっさてん[喫茶店]
This can be used with suru する to say to invite.
Also: to be defeated makeru まける[負ける] (I)
Also: to find mitsukeru みつける[見つける] (I)
The kanji is also used in cyberspace language to mean LOL.
Also: to be alive ikiru いきる[生きる] (I)
Also: to opine omou おもう[思う] The word opine is not often used in English, but this verb is used when you want to give your opinion.
Also: to consider kangaeru かんがえる[考える] (I) Use this verb when you want to say think about.
Also: akeru あける[開ける]
Also: shimeru しめる[閉める]

Also: hajimeru はじめる[始める] This verb has some idiomatic usages. Hajime はじめ[始め] is a noun that means beginning. Hajimete はじめて[初めて] means for the first time. Notice the different kanji. This is often combined with the Te-Form of verbs to say that you are doing something for the first time.
Also: tomeru とめる[止める]
Also: naraberu ならべる[並べる]
Also: to bend magareru まがれる[曲がれる]
Also: to raise ageru あげる[上げる] The more common meaning of ageru あげる[上げる] is actually to give. This word comes from someone kneeling and offering something up to someone.
Also: to give birth umu うむ[生む] This verb is a bit special, because to be born is the passive form of to give birth.
Also: to cross over wataru わたる[渡る]
Also: to come back kaeru かえる[返る] Note that this verb can also be translated as to return.

english: to hang / to splash / to require
notes: Also: to splash; to require kakaru かかる[掛かる]; kakeru かける[掛ける] (UK)
Also: to risk kakeru かける[賭ける] (I)

english: to be the work of / to be the result of / to be done by
notes: Also: to be the result of; to be done by kakaru かかる[係る]

english: to be worried about ki ni
notes: This is an idiomatic phrase.
Also: to start torikakaru とりかかる[取り掛かる] (UK) This is often paired with shigoto しごと[仕事] in the phrase: get to work.
This usually just translates to talk, but has the implied meaning that you went up to someone and started talking to them.
Also: to heal naosu なおす[直す; 治す] The first kaji refers to fix and the second to heal. Note that these are the transitive form of the verbs.
Also: to chew kamu かむ[噛む]
The English phrase, How may I help you, will use this verb, not tasukeru たすける[助ける](to help).

english: to get used to / to grow accustomed to
notes: Also: to grow accustomed to nareru なれる[慣れる]
Also: bus) norikaeru のりかえる[乗り換える] (I)
This is used for transferring residential locations as well as job locations, or job departments within a company. It can also mean to shift as is shifting topics, or spread / be contagious.
Also: to get off oriru おりる[下りる; 降りる] (I) The first kanji is for general going down, the second is for getting off trains.

Also: to convert kaeru かえる[変える] (I) This intransitive / transitive pair is a bit irregular, so I included them both. Kaeru かえる[変える] is the transitive version. This word has a sense of changing completely.
Also: to modify henka suru へんかする[変化する] This verb is different in that it has a sense of not changing something completely, but merely making modifications or alterations to it.
Also: to revise aratameru あらためる[改める] (I) This verb has a sense of a new version, replacing something or revising an old thing with something new. The core meaning of the kanji is to reform.
Also: to make progress susumu すすむ[進む]
Also: information houkoku ほうこく[報告]

This word along with the rest of the words today all have the suffix chouちょう[長] which in this context means leader. The words can sometimes be confused with each other, so it's important to emphasize the first kanji's meaning. In this word, the kanji 課 means chapter or section.
The kanji 部 means section or division.
The kanji 所 means place.
The kanji 局 means bureau.
The kanji 社 means company.
The kanji 副 means vice.

english: to bake / to grill / to roast / to burn
notes: Also: to grill; to roast; to burn yaku やく[焼く]
This word can also be used to say that you yourself are getting hot or excited. The transitive version of this verb is wakasu わかす[沸かす]
This refers to purple grapes. Green (muscat) grapes are called masukatto マスカット.
The word poteto ポテト in Japanese means french fries.
This word literally means ball onion. What we usually refer to in English as green onions or scallions are simply called negi ねぎ.
Also: to collect atsumaru あつまる[集まる]

On a cultural note, most Japanese people will buy snacks as souvenirs whenever they travel somewhere, for their friends, family, or co-workers. If you are going to Japan and plan on staying with someone, be sure to bring them a souvenir from your home country. They will really appreciate it!
The air conditioning machine itself is just called an eakon エアコン. This word refers to the idea or process.
This kanji as well as the previous ones are important to learn if you ever hope to operate the controls of a Japanese air conditioner.
This word refers to the physical flame. The idea of a fire is hi ひ[火], which can also refer to a lighter.
Also: journal nikki にっき[日記]
This is not economic interest, but rather, your desire. To say that you are interested in something you will say that interest exists: Kyoumi ga aru きょうみがある[興味がある].
Because businesses are always trying to be polite to their customers, will you most often hear this as okyakusama おきゃくさま[お客様].
This word is most often used to say that you are looking forward to something. When we say this we can either use tanoshimi desu たのしみです[楽しみです] or tanoshimi ni suru たのしみにする[楽しみにする].
Also: core kokoro こころ[心] This refers to your metaphorical heart, not your physical one.

Said by itself, this word means good feeling. When paired with warui わるい[悪い](bad) this has a very negative meaning. It has many translations in this negative context, some of them being: sick, grossed out, creeped out, etc.
Also: note oto おと[音]
This word can also be used in phrases like I have nothing to do with that, by saying that the relationship doesn't exist: kankei ga nai かんけいがない[関係がない].

english: to fail / to make a mistake shippai
notes: Also: to make a mistake shippai suru しっぱいする[失敗する]
The intransitive version is ochiru おちる[落ちる].
Also: to slide suberu すべる[滑る] (G) This can be used as a slang to say fail a test. This word is also the verb for snowboarding.
Also: to bring up sodateru そだてる[育てる] (I) This refers to raising children.
Also: exceed sugiru すぎる[過ぎる] (I)
Careful, this word is a homonym with kami かみ[紙](paper) and kami かみ[神](god).
Also: fur ke け[毛] This is used for animals.

Also: mustache; beard hige ひげ[髯; 髭; 鬚] This word is used for all types of facial hair, with the three kanji representing the three English words respectively. These are probably not the most important kanji to memorize.
This is the body part, not the box of gold.
This is the body part.
This literally means finger circle.
This literally means hand bag.
Also: government seiji せいじ[政治]
This is also the school subject social studies.
Also: liberty jiyuu じゆう[自由]
Also: finance keizai けいざい[経済]
This refers to the organization, not the individual officers.
Also: traffic koutsuu こうつう[交通] This is not used to say the streets are congested, but rather it is the noun referring to the cars and trains.
This is often used as a prefix to other words, and when it is, you will drop the teki てき[的]
Also: clean; pure kirei きれい[綺麗] (Na)(UK)
Also: high; expensive takai たかい[高い] The expensive definition comes from high price.
This refers to short in length or height.
Also: low hikui ひくい[低い] This refers to short as in low to the ground.
Also: cheap yasui やすい[易い; 安い] The first kanji is the meaning easy, the second cheap. The cheap definition comes from easy to buy.
Also: simple kantan かんたん[簡単] (Na)

Also: Na). I/Na A slang version of this word is chicchai ちっちゃい. As a prefix, the kanji can be attached to nouns to say that the noun is a small version, when this happens, it is pronounced as shou しょう[小].

Also: Na). I/Na If you notice, big and small can sometimes be both I-Adjectives and Na-Adjectives. As a non-native speaker, you should always use them as I-Adjectives, however, in certain situations, Japanese speakers with use them as Na-Adjectives.

english: I saw a cute baby on TV
romaji: Terebi de kawaii akachan o mita
hiragana: テレビでかわいいあかちゃんをみた

romaji: Koko wa kirei na onna no hito ga sunde iru
hiragana: ここはきれいなおんなのひとがすんでいる
このふくはたかいです
このえんぴつはみじかい
このつくえはひくいです

english: I like to eat cheap food
hiragana: やすいたべものがすき

romaji: Kore wa kantan na mondai desu
hiragana: これはかんたんなもんだいです
にほんのいえはちいさい

notes: Also: bad taste mazui まずい[不味い]
Be careful with this one. The negative form is sukunakunai すくなくない.

english: a small amount / a little bit
notes: Also: a little bit sukoshi すこし[少し] Just like in English, this is actually not an adjective, but a noun. It can sometimes function like an adjective, but requires no special grammar.
In English, we usually don't say skillful, but rather good.
Again, for unskillful, we will just translate this as bad.
Also: fast hayai はやい[早い; 速い] The first kanji means early, while the second means fast.
Also: late osoi おそい[遅い]

Also: okay; safe daijoubu だいじょうぶ[大丈夫] (Na); heiki へいき[平気] (Na) Daijoubu だいじょうぶ[大丈夫] is the more common word and can be used in more cases. Heiki へいき[平気] is usually only used to refer to yourself, not an object or situation. If we look at the kanji for heiki へいき[平気], the meanings are peace and spirit, so with heiki we are saying that we are at peace.

Also: ii いい[良い] Good is the only adjective with two different forms. Whenever you conjugate it, you will use yoi よい. Any other time, it's up to the speaker's preference. As an adverb, it can mean often in addition to well.
This word is not used for people. If you say it, it will be insulting. Instead you will use words like grandma and grandpa or you can use the word roujin ろうじん[老人](old person).

Also: kind yasashii やさしい[優しい] This word is also a homonym with yasashii やさしい[易しい](easy / simple), which is an alternate pronunciation of yasui やすい[易い](easy / simple). If you hear yasashii, most people will be using it in the context of kind / gentle.
Also: refreshing suzushii すずしい[涼しい]
This word for hot as well as the next word for cold will only be used when you talk about the weather or how your whole body feels. For objects, we will learn different words later.
People also use this word to say shut up. Literally, they are saying: Loud!
Also: calm shizuka しずか[静か] (Na)
Also: terrible hidoi ひどい[酷い] This word is most commonly used to say someone has done something bad.
Also: solid; stiff katai かたい[固い; 硬い; 堅い] The three kanji are for the type of material that is hard. The first kanji meaning is to harden, the second is to be stiff, the third means strict.
Also: 柔らかい] The first kanji refers to the softness as opposed to hardness. The second refers to soft a soft material, like a blanket, or as in tender, like meat, or a flexible person.
Also: shy hazukashii はずかしい[恥ずかしい]
Like a lot of Japanese adjectives dealing with difficulty, this word can also be used to say you are in a bad situation or not doing well.
On a cultural note, the kanji used in this word is used to count to five, because it has five strokes, similar to the four lines with a cross we use in English.
This word can also be the noun difference, when it is, it will be used like a No-Adjective. It is also often attached to words as a prefix and suffix.
This word has the prefix fuふ[不] which is used in many adjectives to convey the opposite meaning. In this case, it is the opposite of benri べんり[便利] (convenient).
Also: weak usui うすい[薄い] This refers to the dilution of something, like a thin soup or a weak coffee.

Also: splendid kekkou けっこう[結構] (Na) This word doesn't actually mean wonderful. It is used in combinations with other adjectives to mean very. It is also used to said you have had enough or satisfied, as in food or drink. Or if you are in a store and a clerk asks if you need help, you can use this word to say you are fine and don't need help.

Also: very; difficult taihen たいへん[大変] (Na) The kanji for this word together mean unusually big. It almost always is used in a negative context. When used by itself, it will mean you are in a difficult or bad situation.
We learned hot and cold before, but this word as well as the next word are used for objects.
Also: warm (object) atatakaiあたたかい[暖かい] Many people will drop the second taた and pronounce this attakaiあったかい.

Te-Iru This is indeed a verb and not an adjective. To say, It is sunny, we say: Harete iru はれている[晴れている] Like most verbs, you can change this into a noun to make a different sentence with the same meaning: Hare desu はれです[晴れです].
To say that it is cloudy, you will almost always use the noun: kumori くもり[曇り].
In Day 31 we learned the word to fall. We use this verb with rain to say It's raining.
To say It's windy, we can use the verb fukuふく[吹く](to blow), or we can say sugoi kaze desu すごいかぜです[すごい風です].
Also: location basho ばしょ[場所] This word is used for physical locations, not abstractly.
Also: spot tokoro ところ[所] This word is more often used abstractly, and is also used in a few grammar constructions, which we will learn in Volume Two.
Green is a relatively recent color in Japanese. Before it's introduction, Japanese people saw blue and green as the same color. This word can also refer to greenery, as in plants.
This refers to a light brown color and literally means tea color. For a darker brown, you can use the katakana word buraun ブラウン.
This word can also mean gloomy or depressing.
Also: next to soba そば[側] (No) This is a noun that means location, but next to some object.
Also: next door to tonari となり[隣] (No)
This refers to a horizontal position or movement. Like laying on your side, or shaking your head side to side.
Also: straight forward massugu まっすぐ[真っ直ぐ] (Na)
Also: over there mukou むこう[向こう] (No)
Also: unpleasant iya いや[嫌] (Na) This word is also used a lot to say no way: iya da いやだ[嫌だ]. But when people say this, it often just sounds like yada やだ.
Also: funny; strange omoshiroi おもしろい[面白い] This word can cause some confusion because it is often used negatively to mean something is strange or weird, so context is important.
Also: durable; strong joubu じょうぶ[丈夫] (Na)
Also: bustling nigiyaka にぎやか[賑やか] (Na)

Special Technically, the definition for this word is popularity. It can be used like a No- or Na-Adjective, but usually it just precedes a noun without a particle. To say something is popular, you will say popularity exists, ninki ga aru にんきがある[人気がある].

Also: at ease yukkri ゆっくり (Special). Special This word is different from osoi おそい[遅い](slow/late) in that it means to do something calmly, the opposite of flustered. It is very special in that it uses the particle toと instead of naな or noの. But, the toと is often dropped in conversation.
Also: elegant rippa りっぱ[立派] (Na)
One exception to this word is when you want to say that you did something by yourself, you will use jibun de じぶんで[自分で]
This adjective comes from the the verb nemuru ねむる[眠る].

english: Oh I see. / I understand now.
notes: Also: I understand now. naruhodo なるほど This is used to say that you now understand information that was previously unclear.
This literally means ten parts. I suppose ten parts is enough for one person?
Said by itself, this is used to express your sorrow, something like That's too bad or What a pity.
Said by itself, this word means long time no see.
Also: kindness shinsetsu しんせつ[親切](Na) This word is both the adjective and the noun.

Also: amazing sugoi すごい[凄い] This is probably the most popular word to use in Japanese to say things like great, awesome, wonderful. If you look it up, the definition says terrible, but it is usually used in a positive sense, just like terrific is a positive word in English that is derived from the negative word terror. It is also combined with other adjectives to mean very.
This word is a bit special because it can sometimes use noの instead of naな, and also sometimes no particle is necessary. However, it's not too hard to remember that the word special is special.
Also: normal futsuu ふつう[普通](No)
This refers to flavor, not a person's temperament.
Also: crazy hen へん[変] (Na)
Also: precious mezurashii めずらしい[珍しい]
This word is usually used for things like nature and art, whereas kireiきれい[綺麗](beautiful) is used for people.
This is almost always paired with the particle niに.
Also: everything subete すべて[全て] (No) This is interchangeable with zenbu ぜんぶ[全部] in many contexts, just as all and every are interchangeable in English.
Also: the rest hoka ほか[他] (No) (UK) This is often paired with the particle niに.
Also: entirely; completely zenzen ぜんぜん[全然] This can be used with both positive and negative sentences, however, when it is used alone, it will mean not at all.
This is often paired with niに to say truly, or as a response to information, as in, Really?
Also: yon よん[四]
Also: nana なな[七]
Also: zero ゼロ

The kanji in this word are quite common. The first kanji means to calculate and is used in many compound words where calculations are done. The second kanji means number and is used in many compound words with numbers.
We can make the compound word addition by combining this word with the kanji to calculate, which creates the word tashizan たしざん[足し算].
This is the same word as to pull. Subtraction is hikizan ひきざん[引き算].
Do you recognize this word? That's right! It's yet another meaning for kakeru! Multiplication is kakezan かけざん[掛け算].
Division is warizan わりざん[割り算].
This can also be used to say that you are alone, as well as the idiomatic expression one by one, hitorihitori ひとりひとり[一人一人].
Also: amount; count kazu かず[数] This is the noun of the previous verb.
Also: figure; digit suuji すうじ[数字] This doesn't refer to the number set itself, but rather figures or summations on documents and things like that.

Also: finance; accountant kaikei かいけい[会計] This can refer to a company's accounts as well as a person who does accounting. This is actually the same word we learned for bill, but with this word, we rarely add the oお prefix.
Also: shares (finance) kabu かぶ[株]
Also: jikan じかん[時間] The first word is the concept of time, used in phrases like at that time, the second is an interval of time, used in phrases like we have time.
This means day of the Sun.
This means day of the Moon.
This means day of fire.
This means day of water.
This means day of trees.
This means day of gold.
This means day of earth.
Also: 昨夜]; sakuban さくばん[昨晩] Again, the second kanji for yuube has an alternate pronunciation, sakuya さくや.
Also: sakunen さくねん[昨年]
Also: ototoshi おととし[一昨年]

With the seasons we can add the word yasumi やすみ[休み] to mean a vacation during that season. For example, natsuyasumi なつやすみ[夏休み](summer vacation). Yasumi is the noun of the verb yasumu やすむ[休む](to rest), and it can mean day off or holiday.
Also: pm gogo ごご[午後] These words can also be used to mean morning and afternoon.
This is used with a negative verb.
Also: soon sorosoro そろそろ This means something will happen soon, any minute now.
Also: instantly; immediately suguni すぐに[直ぐに] In Japanese, when you say this word for soon, you mean very soon.
This word is more along the lines of the English meaning of soon.
This usually refers to the far future, with spaceships and robots.
This usually refers to the foreseeable future.
Also: end; final saigo さいご[最後]
Also: start; beginning saisho さいしょ[最初] This is also used in the idiomatic phrase, first things first: ichiban saisho いちばんさいしょ[一番最初]

english: the past / ancient times / a long time ago
notes: Also: ancient times; a long time ago mukashi むかし[昔] This refers to things that happened long ago. But because time is relative, if it feels like a long time for the speaker, even a few days or weeks can feel like ancient times.
This refers to the more recent past.

This word literally means a train that stops at all stations. When the train operator is speaking, they will use this word, however, on the signs for the next coming train, they sometimes use a different kanji: futsuuふつう[普通] (usual / normal). The reason is because there are two major train operators in Japan - JR and the Metro. They refuse to use the same words and also charge you transferring fees when you switch lines! The next three words are for the different types of trains.

Also: rapid train kaisoku かいそく[快速] Though these are two different words, these trains are faster than local trains, but slower than the express trains. JR will use semi-express while the Metro will use rapid.

This is the fastest of the trains and sometimes requires a premium. However, it is not often, and usually when it does, the train will look different than the other trains, so don't worry about getting on a train without paying enough.
Also: yet (question) mou もう
Also: soon dandan だんだん [段々] (UK)
Also: before; ahead; future saki さき[先] This can be confusing because it has two completely opposite meanings. However, from the tense of the sentence you can understand which meaning it is.
Also: current; present genzai げんざい[現在]
Also: at last yatto やっと
This is the most basic word for recently
Also: recently kono aida このあいだ[この間] This word literally means during this interval.
This word literally means around this time.

english: this time / next time / another time
notes: Also: next time; another time kondo こんど[今度] This can sometimes be confusing because it can refer to an action taking place now, or be used to say to do something another time, so pay attention to the context.
This is another word that means recently. This word explicitly states the the thing has happened recently before. In English we can sometimes explicitly say this by saying just recently.

english: a little while / in a moment
notes: Also: in a moment shibaraku しばらく[暫く] (UK)
You use this word a lot when talking about the eras of Japan, for example, as we learned before, now is the Heisei Era - heisei jidai へいせいじだい[平成時代].
Also: the present day gendai げんだい[現代]
Also: sudden; steep kyuu きゅう[急] (Na) This can mean an urgent matter, as well as a steep hill. The kanji is the same as the word isogu いそぐ[急ぐ](to hurry).
Also: below; under ika いか[以下] This is not used for comparisons. It is most often used in compound words, and to show limits and expectations.
This is also not used for comparisons. An idiomatic usage of this word, is to say it at the end of a speech, or when you are ordering food, to say That's all.
This is used for time intervals to say some event occurred within an allotted time frame.

english: to come / to go / to be
notes: Also: to go; to be irassharu* いらっしゃる (Honorific). Honorific This is the most common honorific word you will hear, perhaps because it has three different meanings. It is the honorific form of kuruくる[来る], iku いく[行く], and iru いる.

english: to come / to go / to be oide ni
notes: Also: to go; to be oide ni naru おいでになる[お出でになる] (Honorific)(UK). Honorific This word is also the honorific form of kuruくる[来る], iku いく[行く], and iru いる, and is interchangeable with the previous word, but it is used less often. One more common usage is to tell someone to follow you or come to you by saying oide おいで[お出で].

Also: to go mairu まいる[参る] (G) (Humble). Humble This word can be confusing because it also has some non-humble homonyms which use the same kanji. They include to be madly in love, to be defeated, to be annoyed.
Humble Just a reminder, this is the humble form of suru する.
Honorific This is where we derive the command ending nasai なさい.

english: to see / to look goran ni
notes: Also: to look goran ni naru ごらんになる[ご覧に成る] (Honorific). Honorific
Honorific

english: to eat / to drink / to receive
notes: Also: to drink; to receive itadaku いただく[頂く] (Humble). Humble This is actually one of the first words we learned, that we use before we eat something, but as you can see, it has other contexts as well. *There's one more thing we have to cover. A few of the words have special conjugations in the Masu-Form. For irassharu いらっしゃる(to come, to go, to be), nasaru なさる[為さる](to do), and ossharu おっしゃる[仰る] (to say), we will drop the ruる and add an iい before adding masu ます. You don't really need to memorize t

english: Never mind. / Don't worry about it.
notes: Also: Don't worry about it. donmai ドンマイ This word comes from don't mind, but is used to tell someone to not worry about something, that it's not important.

Also: high energy hai tenshon ハイテンション People will often drop the hai ハイ and just say tenshon テンション, which, in English, is only used negatively, as in, tension between two people. In Japanese this refers to a high energy or excited state, like a child opening a new gaming system on Christmas morning and becoming crazy with excitement.
This comes from the word mansion in English, but doesn't refer to a large house, but rather a building with multiple apartments or condominiums. These types of buildings are very popular in Japan.
This refers to people on TV, many of whom are famous for nothing other than being on TV.
This word does not refer to a woman's rights advocate, but quite the opposite. It refers to a man who pampers and takes care of women, often financially.

Though the word half is used, it doesn't necessarily mean you are only half mixed race, and usually, it is only used to refer to a mixed race Japanese person. Many Americans are mixed, but this isn't generally used when talking about Americans. Be careful, because the word nyuuhaafu ニューハーフ refers to a transgender man.
This doesn't refer to seals but rather stickers.
Not consent, but rather an electric outlet.
Japanese people will become very confused if you refer to your bicycle as a bike.

While trump is indeed a game you can play with cards, in Japanese it refers to the cards themselves. Congratulations! You've finished the book. So, what to do now? Your goal as a Japanese speaker should be to pass the JLPT (Japanese Language Proficiency Test). There are five levels, N5 to N1, the latter being the most difficult. To work for a Japanese company, you generally need to pass the N2 test. To work as a translator, you generally need to pass the N1 test. Assuming you can use everything
